{{wikidata link|Q11458107}} [[Category: occurred only about a century later, making it a valuable source for understanding the early state’s view of Buddhism. After Prince Shōtoku’s death, in the 32nd year of Empress Suiko’s reign (624 AD), a system for appointing high-Ranking Buddhist officials was established based on the institutions of the Chinese Southern Dynasties. [[:en:Jianzhen|Ganjin]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Religious Law]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458107]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Temple Law''' (寺院法, Jiinhō) refers to the overall legal framework concerning [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]]. When including legal regulations related to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], it is also called '''Jisha Law''' (寺社法, Jishahō) or '''Shaji Law''' (社寺法, Shajihō). == Ecclesiastical and Secular Jurisdictions == The legal authority over religious institutions is broadly divided into two categories: the secular authority exerted by the state and other worldly powers to regulate temples and shrines from the outside, known as '''{{ill|Tōkyōken|ja|統教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (統教権), and the internal authority by which temples and shrines regulate themselves and their members, called '''{{ill|Chikyōken|ja|治教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (治教権). In premodern times, temples and shrines existed as independent authorities in opposition to secular power, and Chikyōken was widely recognized. As a result, premodern temple law largely consisted of regulations based on Chikyōken, and in a narrow sense, temple law sometimes refers only to these internally derived regulations. Originally, in early Buddhist communities, known as {{ill|Sangha|ja|僧伽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=saṅgha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193944}}, Buddhist precepts and regulations established to govern the community were considered the fundamental form of temple law. This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian [[:en:Canon law|Canon Law]], temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-Article Constitution]], allegedly established by [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]], the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Sōniryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]]. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, [[:en:Bettō|Bettō]], and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Sitting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and [[:en:The Wayfarer (novel)|Gyōnin]]. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=honmatsu system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and [[:en:Danka system|Danka System]]. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Constitution of the Empire of Japan]] acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the [[:en:Religious Organizations Law|Religious Organizations Law]] was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)
}}

When including legal regulations related to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}}, it is also called '''Jisha Law''' (寺社法, Jishahō) or '''Shaji Law''' (社寺法, Shajihō). == Ecclesiastical and Secular Jurisdictions == The legal authority over religious institutions is broadly divided into two categories: the secular authority exerted by the state and other worldly powers to regulate temples and shrines from the outside, known as '''{{ill|Tōkyōken|ja|統教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (統教権), and the internal authority by which temples and shrines regulate themselves and their members, called '''{{ill|Chikyōken|ja|治教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (治教権). In premodern times, temples and shrines existed as independent authorities in opposition to secular power, and Chikyōken was widely recognized. As a result, premodern temple law largely consisted of regulations based on Chikyōken, and in a narrow sense, temple law sometimes refers only to these internally derived regulations. Originally, in early Buddhist communities, known as {{ill|Sangha|ja|僧伽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=saṅgha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193944}}, Buddhist precepts and regulations established to govern the community were considered the fundamental form of temple law. This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian {{ill|Canon law|en|Canon law|WD=Q188462|lt=Canon Law}}, temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-Article Constitution}}, allegedly established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Sōniryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}} in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Sitting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and {{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=honmatsu system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458107]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Temple Law''' (寺院法, Jiinhō) refers to the overall legal framework concerning {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=temples}}. When including legal regulations related to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}}, it is also called '''Jisha Law''' (寺社法, Jishahō) or '''Shaji Law''' (社寺法, Shajihō). == Ecclesiastical and Secular Jurisdictions == The legal authority over religious institutions is broadly divided into two categories: the secular authority exerted by the state and other worldly powers to regulate temples and shrines from the outside, known as '''{{ill|Tōkyōken|ja|統教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (統教権), and the internal authority by which temples and shrines regulate themselves and their members, called '''{{ill|Chikyōken|ja|治教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (治教権). In premodern times, temples and shrines existed as independent authorities in opposition to secular power, and Chikyōken was widely recognized. As a result, premodern temple law largely consisted of regulations based on Chikyōken, and in a narrow sense, temple law sometimes refers only to these internally derived regulations. Originally, in early Buddhist communities, known as {{ill|Sangha|ja|僧伽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=saṅgha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193944}}, Buddhist precepts and regulations established to govern the community were considered the fundamental form of temple law. This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian {{ill|Canon law|en|Canon law|WD=Q188462|lt=Canon Law}}, temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-Article Constitution}}, allegedly established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Sōniryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}} in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Sitting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and {{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=honmatsu system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)
}}

{{wikidata link|Q11458107}} [[Category: occurred only about a century later, making it a valuable source for understanding the early state’s view of Buddhism. After Prince Shōtoku’s death, in the 32nd year of Empress Suiko’s reign (624 AD), a system for appointing high-Ranking Buddhist officials was established based on the institutions of the Chinese Southern Dynasties. {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Ganjin}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Religious Law]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458107]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Temple Law''' (寺院法, Jiinhō) refers to the overall legal framework concerning {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=temples}}. When including legal regulations related to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}}, it is also called '''Jisha Law''' (寺社法, Jishahō) or '''Shaji Law''' (社寺法, Shajihō). == Ecclesiastical and Secular Jurisdictions == The legal authority over religious institutions is broadly divided into two categories: the secular authority exerted by the state and other worldly powers to regulate temples and shrines from the outside, known as '''{{ill|Tōkyōken|ja|統教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (統教権), and the internal authority by which temples and shrines regulate themselves and their members, called '''{{ill|Chikyōken|ja|治教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (治教権). In premodern times, temples and shrines existed as independent authorities in opposition to secular power, and Chikyōken was widely recognized. As a result, premodern temple law largely consisted of regulations based on Chikyōken, and in a narrow sense, temple law sometimes refers only to these internally derived regulations. Originally, in early Buddhist communities, known as {{ill|Sangha|ja|僧伽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=saṅgha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193944}}, Buddhist precepts and regulations established to govern the community were considered the fundamental form of temple law. This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian {{ill|Canon law|en|Canon law|WD=Q188462|lt=Canon Law}}, temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-Article Constitution}}, allegedly established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Sōniryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}} in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Sitting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and {{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=honmatsu system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)

As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=honmatsu system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)
}}

{{wikidata link|Q11458107}} [[Category: occurred only about a century later, making it a valuable source for understanding the early state’s view of Buddhism. After Prince Shōtoku’s death, in the 32nd year of Empress Suiko’s reign (624 AD), a system for appointing high-Ranking Buddhist officials was established based on the institutions of the Chinese Southern Dynasties. {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Ganjin}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Religious Law]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]]
'''Temple Law''' (寺院法, Jiinhō) refers to the overall legal framework concerning {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=temples}}. When including legal regulations related to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}}, it is also called '''Jisha Law''' (寺社法, Jishahō) or '''Shaji Law''' (社寺法, Shajihō). == Ecclesiastical and Secular Jurisdictions == The legal authority over religious institutions is broadly divided into two categories: the secular authority exerted by the state and other worldly powers to regulate temples and shrines from the outside, known as '''{{ill|Tōkyōken|ja|統教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (統教権), and the internal authority by which temples and shrines regulate themselves and their members, called '''{{ill|Chikyōken|ja|治教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (治教権). In premodern times, temples and shrines existed as independent authorities in opposition to secular power, and Chikyōken was widely recognized. As a result, premodern temple law largely consisted of regulations based on Chikyōken, and in a narrow sense, temple law sometimes refers only to these internally derived regulations. Originally, in early Buddhist communities, known as {{ill|Sangha|ja|僧伽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=saṅgha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193944}}, Buddhist precepts and regulations established to govern the community were considered the fundamental form of temple law. This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian {{ill|Canon law|en|Canon law|WD=Q188462|lt=Canon Law}}, temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-Article Constitution}}, allegedly established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Sōniryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}} in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Sitting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and {{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=honmatsu system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)
}}

This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian {{ill|Canon law|en|Canon law|WD=Q188462|lt=Canon Law|1=Canon law}}, temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-Article Constitution|1=Seventeen-article constitution}}, allegedly established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms|1=Taika Reform}}, the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Q11387614|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}} in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Zasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Q11448122|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and {{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin|1=The Wayfarer (novel)}}. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=The branch temple system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System|1=Danka system}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan|1=Meiji Constitution}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law|1=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺院法]]
'''Temple Law''' (寺院法, Jiinhō) refers to the overall legal framework concerning {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=temples|1=Buddhist temple}}. When including legal regulations related to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}}, it is also called '''Jisha Law''' (寺社法, Jishahō) or '''Shaji Law''' (社寺法, Shajihō). == Ecclesiastical and Secular Jurisdictions == The legal authority over religious institutions is broadly divided into two categories: the secular authority exerted by the state and other worldly powers to regulate temples and shrines from the outside, known as '''{{ill|Tōkyōken|ja|統教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (統教権), and the internal authority by which temples and shrines regulate themselves and their members, called '''{{ill|Chikyōken|ja|治教権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (治教権). In premodern times, temples and shrines existed as independent authorities in opposition to secular power, and Chikyōken was widely recognized. As a result, premodern temple law largely consisted of regulations based on Chikyōken, and in a narrow sense, temple law sometimes refers only to these internally derived regulations. Originally, in early Buddhist communities, known as {{ill|Sangha|ja|僧伽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=Sangha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193944}}, Buddhist precepts and regulations established to govern the community were considered the fundamental form of temple law. This narrow definition of temple law included both written statutes established by individual sects or temples and customary laws functioning as precedents. Unlike Christian {{ill|Canon law|en|Canon law|WD=Q188462|lt=Canon Law|1=Canon law}}, temple law did not develop into a formalized legal code. Moreover, temple law was divided into regulations governing internal temple affairs ('''{{ill|Jinaihō|ja|寺内法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaihō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and those extending beyond the temple grounds to areas under its control, such as temple towns (寺辺) and manorial estates. == Overview == === Ancient Period === The earliest temple-related legal framework in Japan is considered to be the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-Article Constitution|1=Seventeen-article constitution}}, allegedly established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} in the 12th year of Emperor Suiko’s reign (604 AD). This law emphasized the state's endorsement of Buddhism and the intertwining of Buddhist faith with state formation. Some scholars argue that the text in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} presenting this constitution was later invented, but even if it were, the Nihon Shoki’s Completion tier 3]]
, a Buddhist monk from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], was appointed under this system, marking the beginning of the {{ill|Sōgō|ja|僧官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}} system. Later, during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms|1=Taika Reform}}, the roles of the "Ten Masters" (十師) and "Head of the Law" (法頭) were introduced. The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} later established the {{ill|Sōniryō|ja|僧尼令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōniryō|1=Q11387614|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387614}} (monastic regulations) and, for Shinto shrines, the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}}, thereby formalizing state control over religious institutions. Regulations based on Chikyōken gradually developed under the influence of existing Sangha rules, the monastic regulations of the Taihō Code, and the precepts introduced by Ganjin. The earliest known temple law is the "{{ill|Sange Gakushōshiki|ja|山家学生式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sange Gakushōshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" written by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}} in 819 AD, created as part of his petition to establish an ordination platform at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}. This document not only sought imperial approval for ordination rights but also prescribed ethical guidelines for monks within the temple.
=== Medieval Period === As Japan transitioned into the Heian period, the decline of the Ritsuryō state resulted in the weakening of state-controlled temple laws, leading to an increased reliance on self-governing regulations under Chikyōken. Internal control by temple heads, such as {{ill|Sankan|ja|三綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan|1=Three Essences|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356941}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Zasu|ja|座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zasu|1=Zasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486140}}, became less effective, and temple communities formed distinct social classes, including {{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Q11448122|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}, {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}}, and {{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin|1=The Wayfarer (novel)}}. Many temple rules were decided by congregational deliberations, and a system called "Tadabun no Ri" (多分の理), akin to a supermajority rule, was used for decision-making. As temple laws extended beyond temple grounds, their influence reached temple-controlled towns (寺辺) and estates. By the late Kamakura period, with the intensification of conflicts between temples and the samurai government, temples reinforced their control over manorial estates using temple law as a legal basis. Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=The branch temple system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System|1=Danka system}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan|1=Meiji Constitution}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law|1=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)

Additionally, during this period, no standardized legal format emerged for these laws, resulting in various forms, such as "Okibumi" (置文), "Okitome" (掟), "Shugijisho" (衆議事書), and "Keijō" (契状).
=== Early Modern Period === During the unification wars led by the {{ill|Oda administration|ja|織田政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda administration|1=Oda regime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608382}}, temple independence was curtailed. By the establishment of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a nationwide religious regulatory framework emerged through the enforcement of various {{ill|Temple Edicts|ja|寺院法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Edicts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} and the implementation of the {{ill|Honmatsu System|ja|本末制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmatsu System|1=The branch temple system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520412}} and {{ill|Danka system|en|Danka system|WD=Q17212817|lt=Danka System|1=Danka system}}. The shogunate also issued the "Shosha Negi Kannushi Hatto" (諸社禰宜神主法度) in 1665 to regulate Shinto priests.
=== Modern Period === With modernization, the state assumed final legal authority over religious institutions, and internal temple laws lost legal standing. Early Meiji-era policies, including {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} and state-sanctioned Shinto reforms, significantly impacted Buddhist and Shinto institutions. By 1871, temple lands were seized, and Shinto priests became government-appointed officials. However, by 1884, the state relinquished direct control over religious appointments, and religious organizations were granted limited autonomy. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Constitution of the Empire of Japan|1=Meiji Constitution}} acknowledged restricted freedom of religion, leading Buddhist sects to advocate for legal recognition of temple properties. In 1939, the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|WD=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law|1=Religious Organizations Law}} was enacted, granting legal corporate status to temples while increasing state control. After World War II, religious freedom was reaffirmed under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}, leading to the enactment of the {{ill|Religious Corporations Law|ja|宗教法人法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporations Law|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451965}} in 1951, which remains the foundation of religious administration in Japan today. == References == * Kazuyoshi Sunagawa, "Temple Law" (Social Science Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kashima Research Institute, 1975) * Nobumichi Inaba, "Temple Law" (Historical Studies Encyclopedia Vol. 9, Kōbundō, 2002) * Yoshihide Kiyota, "Jisha Law" (National History Encyclopedia Vol. 7, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986)
}}

{{wikidata link|Q11458107}} [[Category: occurred only about a century later, making it a valuable source for understanding the early state’s view of Buddhism. After Prince Shōtoku’s death, in the 32nd year of Empress Suiko’s reign (624 AD), a system for appointing high-Ranking Buddhist officials was established based on the institutions of the Chinese Southern Dynasties. {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Ganjin|1=Jianzhen}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Religious Law]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
